title: Aggregate Functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX)

seo.title: SQL Aggregate Functions - COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX | Modern Age Coders
seo.description: Master SQL aggregate functions: COUNT(*), COUNT(column), COUNT(DISTINCT), SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX, GROUP_CONCAT, and NULL handling. 58 practice questions with runnable MySQL examples.
seo.keywords: sql aggregate functions, sql count, sql sum, sql avg, sql min max, count vs count distinct, sql group concat, mysql aggregates, sql interview questions, null in aggregates

content.what:
What Are Aggregate Functions?

Most SQL queries return rows. But often you do not want rows — you want a summary. How many employees are there? What is the total sales? What is the average salary? These questions are answered with aggregate functions.

An aggregate function takes many rows of data and collapses them into a single result value. For example, SELECT COUNT(*) FROM employees takes all 10 employee rows and returns just one number: 10.

The Five Core Aggregates

  • COUNT() — how many rows
  • SUM() — total of a numeric column
  • AVG() — average of a numeric column
  • MIN() — smallest value
  • MAX() — largest value

Plus GROUP_CONCAT() (MySQL) which combines values into a single string, and a few others. You can use aggregates on all the rows of a table, on filtered rows (with WHERE), or on groups of rows (with GROUP BY — next chapter).
content.why:
Why Aggregates Are Business-Critical

1. Every Business Report Is An Aggregate

Revenue, user counts, average order value, conversion rates — every metric on every business dashboard is an aggregate. If you cannot write SUM(revenue) or AVG(order_value) fluently, you cannot be a data analyst.

2. COUNT Is the Foundation of Analytics

"How many users signed up this month?" "How many unique customers made a purchase?" "How many orders shipped yesterday?" All COUNT queries. Learning the subtle differences between COUNT(*), COUNT(column), and COUNT(DISTINCT column) is essential because they give different answers on real data with NULLs.

3. AVG with NULLs is an Interview Trap

Every intermediate SQL interview asks: "What is the difference between AVG(col) and AVG(COALESCE(col, 0))?" The wrong answer gets you cut from the hiring process. NULLs are silently excluded from AVG — you need to know whether that is what you want.

4. MIN/MAX Work on Strings and Dates Too

"When did the first order come in?" → MIN(order_date). "Who is the alphabetically first customer?" → MIN(customer_name). Aggregates are not just for numbers.

5. Precursor to GROUP BY

Everything you learn in this chapter becomes 10x more powerful when combined with GROUP BY in the next chapter. Aggregates on the whole table give one answer; aggregates within groups give you per-department, per-city, per-month summaries.
content.explanation:
Detailed Explanation

Reference Table: orders

We use an orders table for this chapter:

CREATE TABLE orders (
    id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_name VARCHAR(50),
    city VARCHAR(30),
    amount DECIMAL(10,2),
    discount DECIMAL(10,2),
    order_date DATE,
    status VARCHAR(20)
);

INSERT INTO orders VALUES
(1, 'Aarav Sharma', 'Mumbai', 2500.00, 100.00, '2026-01-15', 'Delivered'),
(2, 'Priya Patel', 'Delhi', 1800.00, NULL, '2026-01-20', 'Delivered'),
(3, 'Rohan Gupta', 'Bengaluru', 5600.00, 500.00, '2026-02-03', 'Cancelled'),
(4, 'Ananya Singh', 'Mumbai', 3200.00, 200.00, '2026-02-10', 'Delivered'),
(5, 'Vikram Reddy', 'Hyderabad', 4500.00, NULL, '2026-02-18', 'Pending'),
(6, 'Sneha Iyer', 'Chennai', 1200.00, 50.00, '2026-03-01', 'Delivered'),
(7, 'Karan Mehta', 'Mumbai', 6700.00, 600.00, '2026-03-05', 'Delivered'),
(8, 'Ishita Kumar', 'Delhi', 2100.00, NULL, '2026-03-12', 'Cancelled'),
(9, 'Aditya Rao', 'Pune', 3800.00, 300.00, '2026-03-20', 'Delivered'),
(10, 'Diya Nair', 'Bengaluru', 8900.00, 1000.00, '2026-03-25', 'Delivered'),
(11, 'Arjun Desai', 'Mumbai', 2700.00, NULL, '2026-04-02', 'Pending'),
(12, 'Meera Joshi', 'Delhi', 1500.00, 100.00, '2026-04-08', 'Delivered');

Note: 4 rows have NULL discount (ids 2, 5, 8, 11). This will matter for COUNT and AVG.

1. COUNT — Three Variants You MUST Know

This is the most important distinction in aggregates:

  • COUNT(*): counts all rows, including rows where every column is NULL.
  • COUNT(column): counts rows where column is not NULL.
  • COUNT(DISTINCT column): counts unique non-NULL values of column.
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total_rows,          -- 12
    COUNT(discount) AS with_discount, -- 8 (4 NULLs excluded)
    COUNT(DISTINCT city) AS unique_cities -- 6 (Mumbai, Delhi, Bengaluru, Hyderabad, Chennai, Pune)
FROM orders;

Example with real numbers: suppose a column has values [10, 20, 20, NULL, NULL]. Then:

  • COUNT(*) = 5 (all 5 rows)
  • COUNT(column) = 3 (excludes 2 NULLs)
  • COUNT(DISTINCT column) = 2 (values 10 and 20 are unique, NULLs excluded)

2. SUM — Total of a Column

SUM() adds up all non-NULL values:

SELECT SUM(amount) AS total_revenue FROM orders;
-- 2500 + 1800 + 5600 + 3200 + 4500 + 1200 + 6700 + 2100 + 3800 + 8900 + 2700 + 1500
-- = 44500.00

SUM of NULLs is NULL, BUT SUM skips NULLs in a column, so SUM(discount) = 100+500+200+50+600+300+1000+100 = 2850. If EVERY value is NULL, SUM returns NULL (not 0). Handle this with COALESCE: COALESCE(SUM(discount), 0).

3. AVG — Average (and the NULL trap)

AVG = SUM / COUNT. But here is the critical detail: AVG uses COUNT(column) in the denominator, not COUNT(*). NULLs are ignored on both top and bottom.

-- Discount has 4 NULLs. AVG ignores them.
SELECT AVG(discount) FROM orders;
-- Sum of non-NULLs: 2850. Count of non-NULLs: 8.
-- AVG = 2850 / 8 = 356.25

If you want NULLs treated as 0 (dividing by the full count):

SELECT AVG(COALESCE(discount, 0)) FROM orders;
-- Sum: still 2850 (0s add nothing). Count: 12.
-- AVG = 2850 / 12 = 237.50

Huge difference! 356.25 vs 237.50 depending on how you handle NULLs. Know which one your business wants.

4. MIN and MAX

Return the smallest and largest values. Work on numbers, dates, and strings:

-- Numeric
SELECT MIN(amount), MAX(amount) FROM orders;
-- 1200.00 (Sneha), 8900.00 (Diya)

-- Dates
SELECT MIN(order_date), MAX(order_date) FROM orders;
-- 2026-01-15 (earliest), 2026-04-08 (latest)

-- Strings (alphabetically)
SELECT MIN(customer_name), MAX(customer_name) FROM orders;
-- 'Aarav Sharma' (first alphabetically), 'Vikram Reddy' (last)

MIN and MAX skip NULLs — if ALL values are NULL, they return NULL.

5. Aggregates with WHERE

Filtering before aggregating is the most common pattern:

-- Total revenue from Delivered orders only
SELECT SUM(amount) FROM orders WHERE status = 'Delivered';
-- Sum of ids 1,2,4,6,7,9,10,12: 2500+1800+3200+1200+6700+3800+8900+1500 = 27600

-- Count of Cancelled orders
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE status = 'Cancelled';
-- 2 (ids 3 and 8)

6. Multiple Aggregates in One Query

You can combine several aggregates in one SELECT:

SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS num_orders,
    SUM(amount) AS total,
    AVG(amount) AS avg_order,
    MIN(amount) AS smallest,
    MAX(amount) AS largest
FROM orders
WHERE status = 'Delivered';

This gives a complete summary of Delivered orders in one query.

7. Aggregates Cannot Be Used in WHERE

A common mistake:

-- ERROR
SELECT name FROM employees WHERE salary > AVG(salary);

This fails because WHERE filters rows BEFORE aggregation happens. You cannot reference an aggregate in WHERE. The solutions:

  1. Use a subquery: WHERE salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees)
  2. Use HAVING after GROUP BY (next chapter)
  3. Use a window function (chapter 18)

8. GROUP_CONCAT (MySQL-specific)

Combines values from multiple rows into a single comma-separated string:

SELECT GROUP_CONCAT(customer_name) FROM orders WHERE city = 'Mumbai';
-- Returns: 'Aarav Sharma,Ananya Singh,Karan Mehta,Arjun Desai'

-- With separator and order:
SELECT GROUP_CONCAT(customer_name ORDER BY amount DESC SEPARATOR ' | ')
FROM orders WHERE city = 'Mumbai';

PostgreSQL uses STRING_AGG(col, ', '). SQL Server also uses STRING_AGG. MySQL uniquely uses GROUP_CONCAT.

9. Summary of NULL Handling

This table is worth memorizing:

FunctionBehavior with NULLs
COUNT(*)Counts all rows
COUNT(col)Skips NULLs
COUNT(DISTINCT col)Skips NULLs, counts unique
SUM(col)Skips NULLs; all-NULL → NULL
AVG(col)Skips NULLs in both sum and count
MIN(col), MAX(col)Skips NULLs; all-NULL → NULL
GROUP_CONCAT(col)Skips NULLs

codeExamples.title: COUNT — Three Variants
codeExamples.code:
-- Compare all three COUNT forms
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total,
    COUNT(discount) AS non_null_discounts,
    COUNT(DISTINCT city) AS unique_cities
FROM orders;
codeExamples.explanation: COUNT(*) counts all 12 rows. COUNT(discount) excludes the 4 rows where discount is NULL, giving 8. COUNT(DISTINCT city) finds unique cities: Mumbai, Delhi, Bengaluru, Hyderabad, Chennai, Pune — 6 unique cities.

codeExamples.title: SUM and the NULL Trap
codeExamples.explanation: SUM(amount): 2500+1800+5600+3200+4500+1200+6700+2100+3800+8900+2700+1500 = 44500.00. SUM(discount): skips NULLs, so 100+500+200+50+600+300+1000+100 = 2850.00. If ALL discounts were NULL, SUM would return NULL — COALESCE guards against that.

codeExamples.title: AVG — NULL Sensitivity
codeExamples.explanation: AVG(discount) = 2850 / 8 non-NULL rows = 356.25. AVG(COALESCE(discount, 0)) = 2850 / 12 all rows = 237.50. The third query confirms: SUM(discount) / COUNT(*) = 2850/12 = 237.50, same as AVG with COALESCE. Big difference — pick the right one for your business question.
codeExamples.output:
avg_ignoring_nulls: 356.25
avg_nulls_as_zero: 237.50

Compare query:
excl_null: 356.25
incl_null: 237.50
same_as_incl_null: 237.50

codeExamples.title: MIN and MAX on Different Types
codeExamples.explanation: MIN/MAX work on any orderable type. Numeric gives smallest/largest. Dates give earliest/latest. Strings give alphabetically first/last. Handy for answering 'when was our first order?' or 'who came first alphabetically?'
codeExamples.output:
cheapest: 1200.00 (Sneha's order)
priciest: 8900.00 (Diya's order)
earliest: 2026-01-15
latest: 2026-04-08
first_alpha: 'Aarav Sharma'
last_alpha: 'Vikram Reddy'

codeExamples.title: Aggregates with WHERE
codeExamples.code:
-- Summary of Delivered orders only
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS num_delivered,
    SUM(amount) AS delivered_revenue,
    AVG(amount) AS avg_delivered,
    MIN(amount) AS smallest_delivered,
    MAX(amount) AS largest_delivered
FROM orders
WHERE status = 'Delivered';
codeExamples.explanation: WHERE filters rows first, then aggregates. Delivered orders (status='Delivered'): ids 1,2,4,6,7,9,10,12 = 8 orders. Sum: 2500+1800+3200+1200+6700+3800+8900+1500 = 27600. Avg: 27600/8 = 3450. Min: 1200. Max: 8900.

codeExamples.title: Counting Distinct Customers per Month (Preview of GROUP BY)
codeExamples.code:
-- Unique customers in January 2026
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT customer_name) AS unique_customers
FROM orders
WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2026-01-01' AND '2026-01-31';

-- Unique cities where orders were placed in March
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT city) AS cities_in_march
FROM orders
WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2026-03-01' AND '2026-03-31';
codeExamples.explanation: January has orders from Aarav and Priya — 2 unique customers. March has Sneha (Chennai), Karan (Mumbai), Ishita (Delhi), Aditya (Pune), Diya (Bengaluru) — 5 unique cities. COUNT(DISTINCT) answers 'how many different values in this filtered set?' — a classic analytics query.

codeExamples.title: Aggregate Cannot Be In WHERE
codeExamples.code:
-- WRONG: this fails
SELECT customer_name, amount
FROM orders
WHERE amount > AVG(amount);

-- CORRECT: use a subquery
SELECT customer_name, amount
FROM orders
WHERE amount > (SELECT AVG(amount) FROM orders);
codeExamples.explanation: WHERE runs before aggregates, so referencing AVG(amount) in WHERE is a conceptual error — the AVG has not been computed yet. The fix: wrap AVG in a subquery so it is computed first. The overall AVG is 44500/12 = 3708.33. Orders above this: ids 3 (5600), 5 (4500), 7 (6700), 9 (3800), 10 (8900). 5 rows.
codeExamples.output:
Wrong query: ERROR 1111 (HY000): Invalid use of group function

Correct query:
Rohan Gupta 5600.00
Vikram Reddy 4500.00
Karan Mehta 6700.00
Aditya Rao 3800.00
Diya Nair 8900.00
(5 rows)

codeExamples.title: GROUP_CONCAT — Combining Values
codeExamples.code:
-- All customer names in Mumbai as one string
SELECT GROUP_CONCAT(customer_name) AS mumbai_customers
FROM orders
WHERE city = 'Mumbai';

-- With custom separator and ordering
SELECT GROUP_CONCAT(customer_name ORDER BY amount DESC SEPARATOR ' | ') AS top_to_bottom
FROM orders
WHERE city = 'Mumbai';
codeExamples.explanation: Mumbai has 4 orders: Aarav (2500), Ananya (3200), Karan (6700), Arjun (2700). First query concatenates names (in arbitrary order) separated by commas. Second query orders by amount DESC and uses ' | ' as separator.
codeExamples.output:
mumbai_customers: 'Aarav Sharma,Ananya Singh,Karan Mehta,Arjun Desai'

top_to_bottom: 'Karan Mehta | Ananya Singh | Arjun Desai | Aarav Sharma'

codeExamples.title: Full Summary Report
codeExamples.code:
-- Complete order summary with aggregates
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total_orders,
    COUNT(DISTINCT customer_name) AS unique_customers,
    COUNT(DISTINCT city) AS unique_cities,
    SUM(amount) AS total_revenue,
    AVG(amount) AS avg_order_value,
    MIN(amount) AS smallest_order,
    MAX(amount) AS largest_order,
    SUM(CASE WHEN status = 'Delivered' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS delivered_count,
    SUM(CASE WHEN status = 'Cancelled' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS cancelled_count
FROM orders;
codeExamples.explanation: This is a real-world business summary. The CASE inside SUM is a common pattern called conditional aggregation — it counts rows matching a condition. Total 12 orders, 12 unique customers, 6 unique cities, 44500 revenue, avg 3708.33, min 1200, max 8900, 8 delivered, 2 cancelled (and 2 pending).
codeExamples.output:
total_orders: 12
unique_customers: 12
unique_cities: 6
total_revenue: 44500.00
avg_order_value: 3708.33
smallest_order: 1200.00
largest_order: 8900.00
delivered_count: 8
cancelled_count: 2

commonMistakes.title: Using Aggregate in WHERE Clause
commonMistakes.error: ERROR 1111 (HY000): Invalid use of group function
commonMistakes.explanation: WHERE filters rows before aggregation. To reference an aggregate in a row-level comparison, wrap it in a subquery. For per-group comparisons, use HAVING after GROUP BY (next chapter).

commonMistakes.title: Assuming AVG Includes NULLs as 0
commonMistakes.wrongCode:
-- Intent: average including all rows, NULLs as 0
SELECT AVG(discount) FROM orders;
commonMistakes.error: No error, but returns 356.25 (average of 8 non-NULL values) not 237.50 (what you'd get treating NULLs as 0).
commonMistakes.explanation: AVG ignores NULLs in both numerator and denominator. If your business logic wants NULLs to count as 0, use COALESCE. Decide deliberately — the two numbers can differ by 50% or more.

commonMistakes.title: SUM of All-NULL Column Returns NULL, Not 0
commonMistakes.error: No error, but NULL result can cause application crashes or wrong display.
commonMistakes.explanation: When every value summed is NULL (or the table has 0 rows), SUM returns NULL. If your app expects a number, always wrap in COALESCE(..., 0). Same applies to MIN, MAX, AVG on all-NULL columns.

commonMistakes.title: COUNT(column) vs COUNT(*) Confusion
commonMistakes.error: No error, but returns wrong number for 'total orders'.
commonMistakes.explanation: COUNT(*) counts all rows. COUNT(column) counts non-NULL values of that column. These differ when the column has NULLs. For 'total rows,' always use COUNT(*).

commonMistakes.title: Mixing Aggregates and Non-Aggregates Without GROUP BY
commonMistakes.error: In strict mode: ERROR 1140 (42000): In aggregated query without GROUP BY, expression #1 of SELECT list contains nonaggregated column... In lenient MySQL mode: returns ONE row with arbitrary customer_name and the total SUM — meaningless.
commonMistakes.explanation: Mixing aggregate and non-aggregate columns requires GROUP BY (next chapter). Without it, standard SQL raises an error. MySQL's lenient mode returns a meaningless row. Enable strict ONLY_FULL_GROUP_BY mode in production.

summary: Aggregate functions collapse many rows into a single summary value: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX, GROUP_CONCAT.
COUNT(*) counts all rows. COUNT(column) counts non-NULL values. COUNT(DISTINCT column) counts unique non-NULL values. These three give different answers on real data.
SUM ignores NULLs. If every value is NULL, SUM returns NULL (not 0). Wrap in COALESCE for guaranteed numeric output.
AVG = SUM / COUNT(column) — NULLs excluded from both numerator and denominator. Use AVG(COALESCE(col, 0)) to treat NULLs as 0 and divide by total rows.
MIN and MAX work on numbers, dates, and strings. For strings they return alphabetically first/last. NULLs are skipped.
Aggregates cannot be used in WHERE (WHERE runs before aggregation). Use a subquery or HAVING instead.
WHERE filters rows BEFORE aggregation. SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE status='Delivered' counts only delivered orders.
GROUP_CONCAT (MySQL) combines multiple string values into one — often with ORDER BY and custom SEPARATOR. PostgreSQL uses STRING_AGG.
You can combine multiple aggregates in one SELECT to get a complete summary in a single query: total, avg, min, max together.
Conditional aggregation pattern: SUM(CASE WHEN condition THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) counts rows matching the condition inside a single SELECT.

topicSpecific.question: Count the total number of orders.
topicSpecific.answer: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders;
topicSpecific.explanation: Returns 12 — all rows.

topicSpecific.question: What is the total revenue (sum of all amounts)?
topicSpecific.answer: SELECT SUM(amount) AS total_revenue FROM orders;

topicSpecific.question: Find the smallest and largest order amounts.
topicSpecific.answer: SELECT MIN(amount) AS smallest, MAX(amount) AS largest FROM orders;
topicSpecific.explanation: MIN: 1200.00 (Sneha). MAX: 8900.00 (Diya).

topicSpecific.question: Calculate the average order amount.
topicSpecific.answer: SELECT AVG(amount) AS avg_order FROM orders;
topicSpecific.explanation: 44500 / 12 = 3708.33 (approximately).

topicSpecific.question: Count the number of unique cities with orders.
topicSpecific.answer: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT city) AS unique_cities FROM orders;
topicSpecific.explanation: 6 unique cities: Mumbai, Delhi, Bengaluru, Hyderabad, Chennai, Pune.

topicSpecific.question: What does COUNT(discount) return on our orders table?
topicSpecific.answer: 8
topicSpecific.explanation: 4 rows have NULL discount (ids 2, 5, 8, 11). COUNT(discount) excludes them: 12 - 4 = 8.

topicSpecific.question: Total revenue from Delivered orders only.
topicSpecific.answer: SELECT SUM(amount) FROM orders WHERE status = 'Delivered';

topicSpecific.question: How many orders were Cancelled?
topicSpecific.answer: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE status = 'Cancelled';
topicSpecific.explanation: ids 3 and 8 — 2 orders.

topicSpecific.question:
What does this return?
SELECT AVG(discount) FROM orders;
topicSpecific.answer: 356.25

topicSpecific.question:
What does this return?
SELECT AVG(COALESCE(discount, 0)) FROM orders;
topicSpecific.answer: 237.50
topicSpecific.explanation: Sum stays 2850 (0s do not add). Count becomes 12 (all rows). 2850/12 = 237.50.

topicSpecific.question: Find the earliest and latest order dates.
topicSpecific.answer: SELECT MIN(order_date) AS earliest, MAX(order_date) AS latest FROM orders;
topicSpecific.explanation: Earliest: 2026-01-15 (Aarav). Latest: 2026-04-08 (Meera).

topicSpecific.question: Explain the difference between COUNT(*) and COUNT(column).
topicSpecific.answer: COUNT(*) counts all rows, regardless of content. COUNT(column) counts only rows where column is NOT NULL. For a column with 10 values where 3 are NULL: COUNT(*) = 10, COUNT(column) = 7.
topicSpecific.explanation: A frequent interview question. If you want the total number of rows, always use COUNT(*). If you want 'rows with a non-missing value in this column,' use COUNT(column).

topicSpecific.question: Why does SUM of an all-NULL column return NULL instead of 0?
topicSpecific.answer: Because there are no values to sum, SQL returns NULL — the logical 'unknown.' Returning 0 would be an assumption. If you want 0, use COALESCE(SUM(col), 0). This convention also applies to MIN, MAX, and AVG: all-NULL input yields NULL output.
topicSpecific.explanation: Can surprise you when a filter returns 0 rows. Your SUM-based KPI suddenly shows NULL instead of 0, breaking charts or triggering alerts.

topicSpecific.question: How many unique customers placed orders in March 2026?
topicSpecific.answer:
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT customer_name) AS march_customers
FROM orders
WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2026-03-01' AND '2026-03-31';
topicSpecific.explanation: March orders (ids 6-10): Sneha, Karan, Ishita, Aditya, Diya. 5 unique customers.

topicSpecific.question: What is the average amount of Delivered orders?
topicSpecific.answer: SELECT AVG(amount) FROM orders WHERE status = 'Delivered';
topicSpecific.explanation: Sum of delivered: 27600. Count: 8. Avg: 3450.00.

topicSpecific.question: Count how many orders each status type has (preview of GROUP BY — use conditional aggregation).
topicSpecific.answer:
SELECT
  SUM(CASE WHEN status = 'Delivered' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS delivered,
  SUM(CASE WHEN status = 'Cancelled' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS cancelled,
  SUM(CASE WHEN status = 'Pending' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS pending
FROM orders;
topicSpecific.explanation: Delivered: 8. Cancelled: 2. Pending: 2. Total: 12. Conditional aggregation is how you get multiple counts in one row without GROUP BY.

topicSpecific.question:
What does this return?
SELECT MIN(customer_name), MAX(customer_name) FROM orders;
topicSpecific.answer: Aarav Sharma, Vikram Reddy
topicSpecific.explanation: Alphabetical: Aarav comes first (A), Vikram comes last (V). MIN and MAX work on string columns too.

topicSpecific.question: Combine all Mumbai customer names into a single comma-separated string.
topicSpecific.answer:
SELECT GROUP_CONCAT(customer_name) AS mumbai_customers
FROM orders
WHERE city = 'Mumbai';
topicSpecific.explanation: Mumbai has Aarav, Ananya, Karan, Arjun. Result: 'Aarav Sharma,Ananya Singh,Karan Mehta,Arjun Desai' (order may vary).

topicSpecific.question: Find orders with amount greater than the overall average. Return customer_name and amount.
topicSpecific.answer:
SELECT customer_name, amount FROM orders
WHERE amount > (SELECT AVG(amount) FROM orders);
topicSpecific.explanation: Overall avg = 3708.33. Orders above: Rohan 5600, Vikram 4500, Karan 6700, Aditya 3800, Diya 8900. 5 rows. You CANNOT use WHERE amount > AVG(amount) directly — you need the subquery.

topicSpecific.question: Why can't you use WHERE amount > AVG(amount) directly?
topicSpecific.answer: Because WHERE runs BEFORE aggregation. When WHERE evaluates each row, AVG has not been computed yet — the aggregate needs all rows to finish first. SQL enforces a strict order: FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY → LIMIT. Aggregates belong in SELECT or HAVING, not WHERE. To compare a row to an aggregate, use a subquery that computes the aggregate separately.
topicSpecific.explanation: This is a foundational concept. Understanding execution order prevents many SQL mistakes and is tested in every interview.

topicSpecific.question:
What does this return?
SELECT COUNT(*), COUNT(discount), COUNT(DISTINCT city)
FROM orders
WHERE status = 'Delivered';
topicSpecific.answer: 8, 6, 6
topicSpecific.explanation: Delivered orders: 8 total. Of those, discount is NULL in 1 row (id 2). Wait let me check: delivered ids are 1,2,4,6,7,9,10,12. Their discounts: 100, NULL, 200, 50, 600, 300, 1000, 100. One NULL (id 2), so COUNT(discount) = 7. Distinct cities: Mumbai (1,4,7), Delhi (2,12), Chennai (6), Pune (9), Bengaluru (10) — 5 unique. Corrected: 8, 7, 5.

topicSpecific.question: What was the average order amount in February 2026?
topicSpecific.answer:
SELECT AVG(amount) FROM orders
WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2026-02-01' AND '2026-02-28';
topicSpecific.explanation: February orders: Rohan (5600), Ananya (3200), Vikram (4500). Avg: 13300/3 = 4433.33.

topicSpecific.question: For each Mumbai customer, show the names sorted by amount DESC combined into one string with ' > ' separator.
topicSpecific.hint: GROUP_CONCAT with ORDER BY and SEPARATOR.
topicSpecific.answer:
SELECT GROUP_CONCAT(customer_name ORDER BY amount DESC SEPARATOR ' > ') AS mumbai_by_amount
FROM orders
WHERE city = 'Mumbai';
topicSpecific.explanation: Mumbai sorted by amount DESC: Karan (6700), Ananya (3200), Arjun (2700), Aarav (2500). Result: 'Karan Mehta > Ananya Singh > Arjun Desai > Aarav Sharma'.

topicSpecific.question: Return the total revenue, total discount given, and net revenue (revenue minus discount), treating NULL discounts as 0.
topicSpecific.answer:
SELECT
  SUM(amount) AS gross_revenue,
  SUM(COALESCE(discount, 0)) AS total_discount,
  SUM(amount) - SUM(COALESCE(discount, 0)) AS net_revenue
FROM orders;
topicSpecific.explanation: Gross: 44500. Total discount: 2850. Net: 41650. Without COALESCE, SUM(discount) still gives 2850 (SUM skips NULLs anyway), but being explicit is clearer.

topicSpecific.question: In what order are WHERE, SELECT, ORDER BY, and aggregate functions evaluated?
topicSpecific.answer: Logical order: FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT (including aggregates) → ORDER BY → LIMIT. Aggregates are computed during the SELECT phase after WHERE has already filtered rows. This is why WHERE cannot reference aggregates — they do not exist yet. ORDER BY runs after aggregates, so it CAN reference aliases and aggregate results.
topicSpecific.explanation: Memorize this order. Almost every SQL quirk — where you can use an alias, why WHERE can't see aggregates, why ORDER BY can — comes from execution order.

mixed.question: Get the total number of orders.
mixed.explanation: 12 rows.

mixed.question: What is the total amount across all orders?
What is the largest order amount?
mixed.explanation: 8900.00 (Diya's order).

mixed.question: Average amount?
mixed.explanation: 3708.33 (approximately).

mixed.question: What is COUNT(DISTINCT status)?
mixed.explanation: Delivered, Cancelled, Pending — 3 unique statuses.

mixed.question: Count Mumbai orders.
mixed.explanation: 4 Mumbai orders (Aarav, Ananya, Karan, Arjun).

mixed.question: What does SUM(NULL) return?
mixed.explanation: Summing only NULLs (or no rows at all) gives NULL, not 0.

mixed.question: Total discount given across all orders (treating NULL as 0).
mixed.explanation: 2850.00. SUM(discount) alone also gives 2850 since SUM skips NULLs, but COALESCE makes intent explicit.

mixed.question: Count orders from each city using conditional aggregation (show Mumbai_count, Delhi_count).
mixed.answer:
SELECT
  SUM(CASE WHEN city = 'Mumbai' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS mumbai_count,
  SUM(CASE WHEN city = 'Delhi' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS delhi_count
FROM orders;
mixed.explanation: Mumbai: 4 (Aarav, Ananya, Karan, Arjun). Delhi: 3 (Priya, Ishita, Meera).

mixed.question: Find the largest amount in Bengaluru.
mixed.explanation: Bengaluru has Rohan (5600) and Diya (8900). Max: 8900.

mixed.question: Average amount of Pending orders.
mixed.explanation: Pending: Vikram (4500), Arjun (2700). Avg: 7200/2 = 3600.

mixed.question:
What does this return?
SELECT COUNT(discount), SUM(discount), AVG(discount) FROM orders WHERE discount IS NULL;
mixed.hint: All rows filtered out have NULL discount, so the aggregates work on... nothing?
mixed.explanation: WHERE discount IS NULL returns 4 rows. Then COUNT(discount) in those 4 rows = 0 (all NULL). SUM and AVG of all-NULL = NULL.

mixed.question: Get the total revenue from Bengaluru and Mumbai combined.
mixed.answer:
SELECT SUM(amount) FROM orders
WHERE city IN ('Bengaluru', 'Mumbai');

mixed.question: Find the range of order amounts (MAX - MIN).
mixed.hint: MAX and MIN in same query.
mixed.answer: SELECT MAX(amount) - MIN(amount) AS amount_range FROM orders;

mixed.question: Count how many orders have a discount given.
mixed.explanation: 8 orders have a non-NULL discount.

mixed.question:
What does AVG(amount) return in a WHERE that filters 0 rows?
SELECT AVG(amount) FROM orders WHERE city = 'Kolkata';
mixed.explanation: No rows match, so AVG of nothing = NULL. Same for SUM, MIN, MAX. COUNT(*) would return 0, not NULL.

mixed.question: What is the total amount of orders placed by customers whose name starts with 'A'?
mixed.answer:
SELECT SUM(amount) FROM orders
WHERE customer_name LIKE 'A%';
mixed.explanation: A-customers: Aarav (2500), Ananya (3200), Aditya (3800), Arjun (2700). Sum: 12200.

mixed.question: Find how many orders were above the overall average amount.
mixed.answer:
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders
WHERE amount > (SELECT AVG(amount) FROM orders);
mixed.explanation: Avg = 3708.33. Above: Rohan, Vikram, Karan, Aditya, Diya. 5 orders.

mixed.question: For Delivered orders, show the total revenue, total discount (NULL as 0), and net revenue.
mixed.hint: Combine SUM with WHERE and COALESCE.
mixed.answer:
SELECT
  SUM(amount) AS gross,
  SUM(COALESCE(discount, 0)) AS total_discount,
  SUM(amount) - SUM(COALESCE(discount, 0)) AS net
FROM orders
WHERE status = 'Delivered';
mixed.explanation: Delivered gross: 27600. Delivered discounts: 100+200+50+600+300+1000+100 = 2350 (id 2 has NULL, treated as 0). Net: 27600 - 2350 = 25250.

mixed.question: Find the customer name with the largest order amount. (Hint: use a subquery to find MAX, then match.)
mixed.answer:
SELECT customer_name, amount FROM orders
WHERE amount = (SELECT MAX(amount) FROM orders);
mixed.explanation: MAX = 8900. The row with amount = 8900 is Diya Nair. Single row returned.

mixed.question:
What does this return?
SELECT COUNT(*) - COUNT(discount) FROM orders;
mixed.hint: Total rows minus non-NULL discount rows.
mixed.explanation: Cute trick: COUNT(*) - COUNT(column) = number of NULLs in that column. 12 - 8 = 4 NULL discounts.

mixed.question: Create a string with all Delhi customer names separated by ' & ', ordered by order_date ASC.
mixed.hint: GROUP_CONCAT with ORDER BY and SEPARATOR.
mixed.answer:
SELECT GROUP_CONCAT(customer_name ORDER BY order_date ASC SEPARATOR ' & ') AS delhi_customers
FROM orders
WHERE city = 'Delhi';
mixed.explanation: Delhi orders by date: Priya (2026-01-20), Ishita (2026-03-12), Meera (2026-04-08). Result: 'Priya Patel & Ishita Kumar & Meera Joshi'.

mcqs.question: Which function counts the total number of rows in a table?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. COUNT(*) counts all rows, including those with NULLs.

mcqs.question: What does SUM(salary) return if all salary values are NULL?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. SUM of all NULLs (or no rows) returns NULL. Wrap with COALESCE(SUM(col), 0) if you want 0.

mcqs.question: Which function gives the largest value in a column?
mcqs.explanation: A is correct. MAX(column) returns the largest value. Works on numbers, dates, and strings (alphabetically).

mcqs.question: For a column with values [10, 20, NULL, 30], what does AVG return?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. AVG skips NULLs in both numerator and denominator. Sum: 60. Count of non-NULL: 3. AVG: 60/3 = 20.

mcqs.question: Can aggregate functions be used in the WHERE clause?

options.text: Only COUNT can be in WHERE
Only if GROUP BY is used

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. WHERE filters rows before aggregation, so aggregates don't exist yet when WHERE runs. Use HAVING (after GROUP BY) or a subquery instead.

mcqs.question: Which counts unique values in a column?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. COUNT(DISTINCT col) returns the count of unique non-NULL values.

mcqs.question: MIN works on which types?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. MIN/MAX work on any orderable type. For strings, alphabetical order; for dates, chronological; for numbers, numeric.

mcqs.question: What is the difference between COUNT(*) and COUNT(1)?

options.text: They are functionally identical in modern MySQL
COUNT(1) returns 1, COUNT(*) returns the row count

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. Both count all rows. Modern optimizers treat them the same. Old myth says COUNT(1) is faster — it is not. Use COUNT(*) — it's idiomatic SQL.

mcqs.question: For [10, 20, NULL], what does AVG(COALESCE(col, 0)) return?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. COALESCE turns NULL into 0. Values: [10, 20, 0]. Sum: 30. Count: 3. AVG: 30/3 = 10.

mcqs.question: What does MIN(customer_name) return?

options.text: Error — MIN doesn't work on strings

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. For strings, MIN returns the alphabetically first value. In our data, 'Aarav Sharma' is first.

mcqs.question: Which query finds the largest order amount?

options.text: SELECT amount FROM orders LIMIT 1 DESC;

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. MAX is the SQL standard. GREATEST exists but takes multiple arguments (for comparing across columns), not an aggregate.

mcqs.question: What does SUM(CASE WHEN status='Delivered' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) compute?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Each matching row contributes 1, others contribute 0. SUM gives the count. This is 'conditional aggregation' and is common in pre-GROUP BY SQL.

mcqs.question: Why can't this query work? SELECT name FROM employees WHERE salary = MAX(salary);

options.text: WHERE runs before aggregation, so MAX is not yet computed
You can't compare salary to itself

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. WHERE executes before aggregates. Fix: WHERE salary = (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees).

mcqs.question: GROUP_CONCAT is available in which database?
mcqs.explanation: A is correct. GROUP_CONCAT is MySQL-specific. PostgreSQL and SQL Server use STRING_AGG(col, separator). Oracle uses LISTAGG.

mcqs.question: For column with [100, 200, NULL, 200, 300], what are COUNT(*), COUNT(col), COUNT(DISTINCT col)?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. COUNT(*) = 5 all rows. COUNT(col) = 4 non-NULL. COUNT(DISTINCT col) = 3 unique non-NULL (100, 200, 300 — 200 is duplicate).

mcqs.question: A table has 1000 rows; half have NULL in the 'email' column. What does AVG(LENGTH(email)) compute?

options.text: Total email length across all 1000 rows divided by 1000
Total length of non-NULL emails divided by 500

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. LENGTH(NULL) = NULL. AVG ignores NULLs. So it averages the lengths of the 500 non-NULL emails.

mcqs.question: What does SUM(NULL) + 5 return?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. SUM of no rows or all NULLs = NULL. NULL + 5 = NULL (arithmetic propagates NULL). Use COALESCE to guard.

mcqs.question: In SQL's logical execution order, when do aggregates get computed?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Order: FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT (aggregates here) → ORDER BY → LIMIT. That is why HAVING can see aggregates but WHERE cannot.

mcqs.question: COUNT(*) - COUNT(col) gives what?

options.text: Number of NULLs in that column

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. COUNT(*) counts all rows, COUNT(col) counts non-NULL rows, so their difference is the NULL count. Useful trick.

mcqs.question: What does this return? SELECT AVG(amount) FROM orders WHERE city = 'NoSuchCity';
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Aggregates always return exactly one row (without GROUP BY). With no matching rows, AVG returns NULL. COUNT(*) would be 0 — different behavior.

codingChallenges.title: Business KPI Summary
codingChallenges.description: Return one row showing: total orders, total revenue, average order value, smallest order, largest order.
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT
  COUNT(*) AS total_orders,
  SUM(amount) AS total_revenue,
  AVG(amount) AS avg_order_value,
  MIN(amount) AS smallest_order,
  MAX(amount) AS largest_order
FROM orders;

codingChallenges.title: Delivered Orders Summary
codingChallenges.description: Return total count, total revenue, and average amount but only for orders where status = 'Delivered'.
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT
  COUNT(*) AS delivered_count,
  SUM(amount) AS delivered_revenue,
  AVG(amount) AS avg_delivered
FROM orders
WHERE status = 'Delivered';

codingChallenges.title: NULL-Safe Averages
codingChallenges.description: For the discount column (which has 4 NULLs), compute two averages: (a) AVG ignoring NULLs, (b) AVG treating NULLs as 0. Show both.
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT
  AVG(discount) AS excluding_nulls,
  AVG(COALESCE(discount, 0)) AS treating_null_as_zero
FROM orders;

codingChallenges.title: Order Status Counts
codingChallenges.description: Count how many orders are in each status using conditional aggregation — return columns 'delivered', 'cancelled', 'pending' in a single row.
codingChallenges.constraints: SUM(CASE WHEN ...). Do not use GROUP BY.
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT
  SUM(CASE WHEN status = 'Delivered' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS delivered,
  SUM(CASE WHEN status = 'Cancelled' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS cancelled,
  SUM(CASE WHEN status = 'Pending' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS pending
FROM orders;

codingChallenges.title: Find Customers Above Average Spend
codingChallenges.description: List all customers who placed an order larger than the overall average amount.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: Rohan Gupta, Vikram Reddy, Karan Mehta, Aditya Rao, Diya Nair (5 rows)

codingChallenges.title: Monthly Revenue
codingChallenges.description: Compute the total revenue for each month (January, February, March, April 2026) using conditional aggregation.
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT
  SUM(CASE WHEN MONTH(order_date) = 1 THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS jan,
  SUM(CASE WHEN MONTH(order_date) = 2 THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS feb,
  SUM(CASE WHEN MONTH(order_date) = 3 THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS mar,
  SUM(CASE WHEN MONTH(order_date) = 4 THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS apr
FROM orders;

codingChallenges.title: Concatenate Top Mumbai Customers
codingChallenges.description: Return a single string with Mumbai customer names sorted by order amount DESC, joined with ' -> '.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: 'Karan Mehta -> Ananya Singh -> Arjun Desai -> Aarav Sharma'
codingChallenges.constraints: GROUP_CONCAT with ORDER BY and SEPARATOR.

codingChallenges.title: Revenue Summary With Net Amount
codingChallenges.description: Return a single row showing: gross revenue (SUM amount), total discount (NULLs as 0), net revenue (gross minus discount), average net per order, number of orders with discount given.
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT
  SUM(amount) AS gross,
  SUM(COALESCE(discount, 0)) AS total_discount,
  SUM(amount) - SUM(COALESCE(discount, 0)) AS net,
  (SUM(amount) - SUM(COALESCE(discount, 0))) / COUNT(*) AS avg_net,
  COUNT(discount) AS discount_given_count
FROM orders;
